findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as PROBLEM
findings.suggestion: You've got the problem statement buried in your startup information section on slide 2, but investors need it as its own dedicated slide early in your deck—ideally right after your cover slide. Pull out the core pain points (manual processes, delayed lead responses, low conversion rates, lack of data-driven insights for foreign investors) and create a visual problem slide that shows the friction in the current buyer journey, maybe with a stat like "70% of property searches in UAE still rely on manual agent follow-up" to make it visceral before you jump into your solution.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as SOLUTION
findings.suggestion: You've got your problem statement and business model covered, but you're missing a dedicated slide that walks investors through exactly how MIRA works from the customer's perspective—a visual step-by-step of the user experience. Right now your "Product/Technology Solution" slide is dense and feature-heavy; create a separate "How It Works" slide showing the six-step journey (Discovery → Matching → Valuation → Virtual Exploration → Conversion → After-Sales) with simple visuals or screenshots, so investors can instantly see the flow and understand why customers would choose MIRA over a traditional agent or competitor platform.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 8, Slide 9, Slide 10

findings.label: Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as FINANCIALS
findings.suggestion: You've got strong early traction with AED 800K in revenue and AED 50M+ in transaction volume, but investors need to see the unit economics behind those numbers—add a slide showing your CAC, LTV, and payback period broken down by revenue stream (commission share vs. developer partnerships vs. SaaS licensing), along with a 24-month cash burn and revenue projection so they understand how this capital gets you to profitability or the next milestone. Right now it looks impressive in isolation, but without the financial narrative connecting spend to returns, they'll struggle to justify the valuation.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 6

findings.label: Does the deck include a Market slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as MARKET
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing the UAE real estate market size, growth rate, and your addressable opportunity within it—right now investors only see a generic "$280B global commission pool" with no grounding in your actual market. For example: "UAE real estate transaction volume: AED 500B+ annually (2023), with off-plan segment growing 25% YoY; MIRA targets 2% of developer-facilitated deals in Year 3 = AED 2.5B TAM, representing AED 75-125M revenue opportunity at 3-5% commission."

findings.label: Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as COMPETITION
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide mapping MIRA against 3-4 specific competitors in UAE/MENA—name them (Property Finder, Bayut, Huspy), show side-by-side comparison of features (AI lead qualification, AR/VR, developer integrations, pricing model), and explicitly state what MIRA does differently and why customers would switch. Your diligence report already flags this as a critical gap, so investors expect you to own this head-on rather than hoping they won't notice the crowded landscape.

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
findings.evidence: Deck has 11 slides (minimum 8)

findings.label: Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?
findings.evidence: Revenue/currency figures found

findings.label: Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: LLM returned non-JSON after retry
findings.suggestion: You're listing "AR/VR + NLP + CRM" as your tech stack, but investors will ask what's actually proprietary here—these are all off-the-shelf components. Add a dedicated technical architecture slide that shows: (1) which LLM you're using and what custom fine-tuning or prompt engineering differentiates your AI responses in real estate scenarios, (2) how your AR/VR layer uniquely solves the property visualization problem versus competitors like Matterport, and (3) what data moats or algorithmic advantages you've built on top of the CRM integrations—for example, "proprietary price-forecasting model trained on 50K+ UAE transactions

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: unclear if MIRA has strong defensible IP or first-mover advantage
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide on IP and defensibility that spells out exactly what's proprietary to MIRA — for example, your custom emotional intelligence layer trained on 10,000+ real estate conversations (vs. off-the-shelf LLMs), your proprietary dataset of Dubai property valuations built from DLD integrations, or exclusive partnership agreements with specific developer networks that competitors can't easily replicate. Right now investors can't tell if you've built something hard to copy or if a well-funded competitor could launch the same thing in six months.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: What is the current development status of MIRA's AI/AR platform? Is the technology live, beta, or roadmap?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your Technology slide stating exactly where MIRA stands: "Platform Status: Live with 15+ active agents in UAE pilot (Q4 2024)" or whatever your actual stage is—beta, MVP in production, full launch, etc. Right now you're claiming "strong traction and revenue" but investors can't tell if you're running a controlled pilot, a scaled operation, or still building core features, which makes them question whether those 70% conversion and 90% satisfaction metrics are real or theoretical.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Can you provide demonstrations or live access to the full customer journey, showcasing all claimed functionalities?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that walks through a real customer transaction from start to finish—show exactly how a buyer discovers a property through MIRA, gets an AI-generated ROI forecast with AR visualization, receives financing guidance, and closes the deal, with actual screenshots or a recorded demo showing each step and the specific outputs (e.g., "AI identified 3 comparable properties and flagged 12% upside potential"). This turns your claimed "full-stack service" from abstract into concrete proof that the platform actually delivers what you're pitching.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Is the AI model proprietary or built using third-party LLMs? Is there any IP protection?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated technical architecture slide that explicitly states whether your AI model is proprietary, fine-tuned, or built on third-party LLMs (like OpenAI/Claude), and map out your IP strategy—for example: "Core NLP engine: proprietary fine-tuning on [LLM provider] with custom real estate training data; AR visualization: in-house development; CRM integration layer: licensed APIs with vendor agreements in place." This directly answers the investor question sitting in your diligence notes and removes the ambiguity that's currently flagged as a competitive risk.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: How is data privacy and security ensured, especially for high net worth and institutional clients?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated security & compliance section that names specific certifications MIRA holds (ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR compliance, UAE DPA registration) and describes your data handling architecture for high-net-worth clients—for example: "Client financial data and property portfolios are encrypted end-to-end, stored in UAE-regulated data centers, with role-based access controls limiting agent visibility to assigned properties only." This directly addresses investor concern about institutional trust and differentiates you from competitors who haven't invested in compliance infrastructure.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Please provide CAC vs. LTV data per customer type (B2B and B2C).
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing your unit economics split by B2B (developer partners) and B2C (individual buyers): for each segment, show how much you spend to acquire one customer (CAC), what lifetime value they generate (LTV), and your payback period — for example, "B2B Developer CAC: AED 5K | LTV: AED 120K | Payback: 2 months" versus "B2C Buyer CAC: AED 800 | LTV: AED 8K | Payback: 4 months." This tells investors immediately which segment is more efficient and sustainable.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: What proportion of claimed AED800K revenue is recurring, and what is derived from one-off deals?
findings.suggestion: Break down your AED800K revenue into two clear numbers on the slide: how much is recurring monthly/annual contracts (with customer names or categories if possible) versus one-off project fees, then add a simple table showing your top 3-5 paying customers, their contract value, and contract length — this instantly proves you have real paying customers, not just pilots, and shows investors the revenue quality they're asking about.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: What is your go-to-market strategy in neCw geographies (e.g., expansion to Saudi Arabia, UK)? How do you plan to acquire customers in these markets – organic vs. paid strategies? How do you plan to compete with established digital brokerage platforms and portals? Is there an internal sales team oOr partner-led channel strategy?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing questions investors have, not answering them—flip this into a concrete GTM slide that shows exactly how you'll win in Saudi Arabia and the UK. For example: "Saudi Arabia: Partner with Emaar and Damac (signed LOI pending) to embed MIRA into their agent portals, targeting 500 agent signups in year one via co-marketing; UK: Direct B2B sales to PropTech platforms like Rightmove, starting with a pilot with [specific partner name] at £X CAC vs. £Y LTV." This specificity—real partners, real numbers, real channels—is what separates a plan from a pitch.

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple chart or table showing your AED800K revenue claim with month-over-month growth rates for the last 6-12 months—for example, "Month 1: AED50K → Month 6: AED180K (260% growth)" alongside the recurring vs. one-off breakdown investors asked about. This turns your traction claim from a single number into proof of momentum and directly answers the financial credibility questions on this slide.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: Please provide customer feedback, case studies, or NPS scores from early adopters.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing 2-3 specific customer wins with their names, logos, and one concrete metric per customer—for example, "Emaar Properties: 340 qualified leads generated in 60 days" or "Damac Developer: 28% conversion rate improvement on property inquiries." Even if you can't share logos publicly yet, naming the tier of customer (top 5 UAE developer, international brokerage firm) and their measurable outcome will make your AED800K revenue claim credible and give investors confidence you've solved a real problem.

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong domain expertise across fundraising, strategy, and governance, but you're missing someone who owns the day-to-day execution of your core platform—right now it reads like a collection of services without a clear product leader steering the ship. Add a Chief Product Officer or Head of Platform to your core team (separate from your advisor network) with specific experience building marketplace or community platforms, ideally someone who's scaled a hub-and-spoke model before, so investors see who's actually building the infrastructure that connects all these moving pieces.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now your team slide shows expertise areas but no proof that your founders have actually built or scaled anything themselves—investors need to know why you're qualified to run this company, not just what services you offer. Add a brief line under each founder's name showing one concrete win: for example, "Shirin: Built HR tech platform to 500+ users before joining" or "Hazem: Scaled marketing function at [Company] from 2 to 15 people," so investors see you've walked the walk in your own domain.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual experts listed, but investors need to see who's actually guiding your company's direction—add a dedicated "Board of Advisors" or "Key Advisors" section that names 3-5 people (ideally with their company affiliations or past exits) who are actively advising Deal360 itself, not just available for mentoring startups. For example: "Led by advisors including [Name], former CFO of [major regional bank], and [Name], founder of [successful UAE startup]"—this signals that experienced operators believe in your model enough to commit their time to your success.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple one-liner to your team slide stating the founder equity split and vesting schedule—something like "Founders: 60% equity vesting over 4 years with 1-year cliff" positioned near your leadership names. Investors need to see this upfront to understand incentive alignment and commitment, and it signals you've thought through cap table structure from day one.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service. Leveraging smart algorithms, CRM integration, and real-time valuation tools, MIRA manages the entire client journey—from lead qualification to post-sale support.
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide on regulatory compliance and licensing that spells out exactly which UAE real estate and AI regulations MIRA operates under, what licenses you currently hold (e.g., RERA registration if applicable), and what approvals you've already secured from Dubai Land Department or other authorities. For example: "MIRA operates as a technology platform under UAE regulations [cite specific law], holds [X license type] from [authority], and has received written approval from DLD for API integration with their property database—see attached MOU dated [date]." This removes a major investor concern flagged in your own diligence report and shows you've thought through compliance from day one, not as an afterthought.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide addressing your legal and regulatory standing in the UAE and any markets you're expanding to—specifically: which real estate and AI agent licenses you hold or are pursuing, any pending regulatory approvals needed to operate MIRA, and confirmation that you've resolved (or are actively managing) compliance with DLD rules, data privacy laws, and brokerage regulations. For example: "MIRA Regulatory Status: UAE Real Estate Brokerage License (pending Q4 2025) | ADIB Data Privacy Compliance (certified) | AI Agent Framework Alignment (in progress with DFSA)" — this shows investors you're not flying blind on compliance, which is critical in a regulated industry like real estate

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Good news—your numbers are internally consistent across the slides, so you don't have a mathematical contradiction problem to fix. However, you're missing a critical bridge: the traction metrics on Slide 4 (AED 50M in transactions, AED 800K revenue) don't connect to a clear financial projection, which investors will immediately ask for. Add a simple one-page financial forecast showing how you get from AED 800K revenue today to your break-even and profitability timeline, broken down by revenue stream (commission share, developer partnerships, SaaS licensing, etc.)—this transforms impressive early numbers into a believable growth story rather than leaving them as isolated data points.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service... This automation and intelligence is intended to disrupt local and global real estate markets by delivering scalable, data-driven services that traditional agents cannot match

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo, hllooerggeyyin AA, inns nninuuteaanll dRReeedpp sooolrrettly
findings.suggestion: You've got corrupted text scattered throughout your footer — that garbled string "TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo..." appears on nearly every slide and completely undermines your professionalism. Replace it with a clean, single-line legal disclaimer like "This document is confidential and for authorized recipients only" or remove it entirely if it's not essential. This one fix will instantly make your deck look polished instead of broken.
